text.content: MINI PRD: P01 — FluidFlow Product Overview
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P1 | Type: Pillar Page | Word Target: 3000-5000
What This Article Does Now

Lists all five modules with named correlations, auto-sizing capabilities, and standards compliance. Decent technical specificity — correlations are named, not generic. ~435 words, far below pillar target. No standard opening statement, no "So What?" closing, no Quick Reference table, no glossary links, no internal linking.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the primary pillar page for the entire KB — the page evaluating engineers land on. Must open with the engineering problem (sizing and analyzing pipe systems), name FluidFlow's scope in 2-3 sentences, then walk through capabilities with enough depth to build trust. End with a Quick Reference summary table and a "So What?" that connects to business outcomes.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering problem FluidFlow solves, what the reader will understand after reading, and name FluidFlow directly. No preamble.
**Expand to pillar depth** (~3,000 words): Each module section needs 1-2 paragraphs of engineering context before the bullet lists. Explain WHY each capability matters, not just WHAT it is.
**Add Quick Reference table**: Summary of all modules with their key correlations, standards, and use cases in a scannable table.
**Add Best Practices section**: Practical advice for new evaluators — which module to start with, how to validate results, common first-project mistakes.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting FluidFlow to business outcomes (faster design, fewer errors, standards compliance).
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
**Fix "Heat Transfer" section**: Remove "radiation models" — FluidFlow does conduction/convection for pipes, not radiation. Verify against spec sheet.
**Fix "Dynamic Analysis" section**: Clarify that Scripting Module provides "light" dynamic analysis — full transient is FlowDesigner (not yet released). Do NOT claim full transient capability.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in Pumps auto-sizing bullet
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in Usability section (interactive charts)
[Economic Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#economic-velocity) — in Pipes auto-sizing bullet
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in Best Practices (validation)
[Deposition Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#deposition-velocity) — in Slurry module section
Internal Links
UP: This IS the pillar — no upward link needed
SIDEWAYS: P02 Build Your First Network, P11 Designer Handbook, P13 Quality Assurance & Standards, P44 Relief Valve Sizing
DOWN (glossary): Duty Point, System Curve, Economic Velocity, NPSH, Deposition Velocity
BLOG: Link to most relevant blog post on fluidflowinfo.com/blog/ (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

6 — "Works across every flow regime" — weave into the opening statement naturally: FluidFlow handles liquid, gas, two-phase, slurry, and non-Newtonian from a single interface. Do not repeat as a separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
All 5 modules (Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase, Slurry, Scripting)
All pressure drop correlations (4 liquid, 8 two-phase, 8 slurry)
All auto-sizing methods (Pipes, Pumps, Relief, CV, Fans, Orifice)
Standards: API RP 520, ISO 4126-1, ISO 5167, ANSI/ISA-75.01.01
Databases: 1,283 fluids, 14 pipe types, 118 alerts
Fitting methods: Idelchik, Miller, Crane, SAE
MINI PRD: P02 — Build Your First Network in FluidFlow
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P1 | Type: Getting Started | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Six-step walkthrough from blank workspace to calculated model. References YouTube and Loom tutorials. Good practical flow with clear steps. ~390 words — at the low end. Best Practices section is generic. No opening statement, no "So What?", no Quick Reference, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Be the definitive "first 15 minutes" guide — the article linked from every CTA that says "get started." Opening statement should name the problem (engineers need to go from blank screen to working model fast), state the outcome (a calculated, validated network), and set the expectation (completable in under 15 minutes).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "FluidFlow lets you build a complete pipe network model from scratch in minutes. This guide walks you through placing components, connecting pipes, defining properties, and running your first calculation — you'll have flow rates, pressures, and velocity results in under 15 minutes."

**Add engineering context to each step**: Brief WHY before each HOW. E.g., Step 1 — "Every model starts with boundary conditions because FluidFlow needs to know where fluid enters and exits the system."
**Add "What you'll need" prerequisites callout**: FluidFlow installed and activated, a simple system in mind (e.g., pump feeding a tank through 50m of pipe).
**Expand Best Practices**: Replace generic bullets with specific advice tied to steps (e.g., "Start with Known Pressure boundaries — they're more forgiving for first models than Known Flow").
**Add Quick Reference**: Table summarizing the 6 steps with key action and shortcut for each.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic connecting first model to engineering confidence.
**Add glossary links** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars.
**Add screenshot placeholders**: [Screenshot: blank flowsheet], [Screenshot: component palette], [Screenshot: calculated model with flow arrows].
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Flowsheet](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#flowsheet) — Step 1
[Boundaries](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#boundaries) — Step 2 (Best Practices)
[Data Palette](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#data-palette) — Step 6
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — Step 6 (Chart tab)
[Pump Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#centrifugal-pump-performance-curve) — Step 6
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P03 Quick Start Guide, P04 UI Overview, P07 How to Build a Model
BLOG: 1 relevant blog post
CTA: Free Training — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
7 — "Short learning curve, deep capability" — reinforce in the "So What?" closing: productive in minutes, capable of complex multi-phase systems as you grow.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Components: Boundaries (Pressure, Flow, Tank), Pipes, Pumps
Auto-sizing mention (Generaux economic velocity)
Reporting: Charts (system curve, pump curve)
MINI PRD: P03 — Quick Start Guide: Build Your First Model in Minutes
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P1 | Type: Getting Started | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Condensed 5-step version of P02. Has pro tips (Shift-select, composite charts). ~298 words — too thin. References a system diagram but no image. Missing opening statement, glossary links, "So What?", Quick Reference, internal links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the fast-track alternative to P02 for experienced engineers who want to evaluate FluidFlow without reading a full tutorial. Completable in under 10 minutes. Should complement P02, not duplicate it — P03 is the "I already know hydraulics, show me the software" path.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement**: Name the problem (engineers evaluating software need to see results fast), state the outcome (a working model in 5 steps), name FluidFlow.
**Add the system diagram**: [Screenshot needed: simple pump-pipe-tank system used in the tutorial]. The article references "a simple system shown below" but no image exists.
**Differentiate from P02**: Add a callout at the top: "For a detailed walkthrough, see Build Your First Network. This guide is the fast track."
**Expand each step slightly** to 800+ words total: Add 1 sentence of engineering context per step.
**Add Quick Reference**: 5-step summary table with keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+F8 for calculate, F8 for visible results).
**Add "So What?" closing**: "Building your first model in minutes proves the interface won't slow you down — the depth comes when you need it."
**Add glossary links** (see below).
**Add meta description**.
**Fix Step 5 link**: "Energy & Hydraulic Grade Lines (EGL/HGL)" should link to P32/P33.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Flowsheet](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#flowsheet) — Step 1
[Component](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#component) — Step 3
[Input Editor](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#input-editor) — Step 3
[Data Palette](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#data-palette) — Step 5
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P02 Build Your First Network, P04 UI Overview, P07 How to Build a Model
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
3 — "Fastest path from problem to answer" — naturally fits this article's purpose. Weave into opening statement.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Components: Boundaries, Pipes, Pumps
Reporting: Charts, EGL/HGL
MINI PRD: P04 — FluidFlow User Interface Overview
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P2 | Type: Getting Started | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Covers 8 UI areas (title bar, toolbars, flowsheet, component palette, input editor, data palette, status bar). References YouTube. Adequate structure. ~427 words. Missing opening statement, "So What?", Quick Reference, screenshots, glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Be the visual reference guide engineers return to when they can't find a feature. Screenshots are essential — this is the ONE article where images carry more weight than text. Should orient a new user in under 5 minutes.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "FluidFlow's interface is built around a central flowsheet with input tools on one side and results on the other. This guide maps every panel so you can find any feature in seconds."

**Add screenshot placeholders for every UI area**: [Screenshot: full UI with numbered callouts 1-8], [Screenshot: Component Palette detail], [Screenshot: Data Palette tabs], [Screenshot: Input Editor with a pump selected].
**Add Quick Reference**: Table of keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+F8 calculate, F8 visible results, F9 units, F2 calc options, Ctrl+R rotate, Ctrl+Del delete).
**Add "So What?" closing**: "Knowing the interface means you spend time engineering, not searching for buttons."
**Expand each section** with 1-2 sentences of practical context (e.g., "The Messages tab is where FluidFlow displays its 118 design alerts — check here first after every calculation").
**Add glossary links** (see below).
**Add meta description**.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Flowsheet](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#flowsheet) — Section 3
[Data Palette](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#data-palette) — Section 7
[Input Editor](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#input-editor) — Section 6
[Component](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#component) — Section 5
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P02 Build Your First Network, P07 How to Build a Model, P10 How to Analyze Results
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Build Your First Network — https://knowledge.fluidflowinfo.com/en/articles/13238470-build-your-first-network-in-fluidflow
Selling Message
0 — No selling message fits naturally in a UI reference guide. Let the screenshots speak.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Reporting: 118 alerts, Excel/PDF/BOM
Components: all junction type categories (for Component Palette description)
Databases: 1,283 fluids, 14 pipe types (for database mention)
MINI PRD: P05 — FluidFlow Installation & Activation Guide
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P2 | Type: Getting Started | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

7-step process covering install, activation methods (License Manager, email, alternative). Good detail on FF3SETUP.EXE, Citrix/Terminal Services. ~446 words. Missing opening statement, "So What?", Quick Reference, glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Get a new user from download to activated software with zero friction. Should be completable in under 10 minutes. The Quick Reference should be a decision tree: "Which activation method should I use?"
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "FluidFlow installs from a single executable and activates in under 5 minutes. This guide covers every activation method — from automatic license manager to email-based activation for restricted environments."
**Add Quick Reference**: Decision tree table — "If you have internet → License Manager | If firewall blocks → Email activation | If network deployment → Network Installation PDF".
**Add "So What?" closing**: "A clean installation means you can start modeling immediately — no time lost to setup."
**Add troubleshooting callout**: What to do if activation fails (common: no read/write access to install folder).
**Add meta description**.
**Remove hedging language**: Replace "The FluidFlow software installation process is straightforward and flexible" with direct statement of what the reader will accomplish.
**Add link to P06** (System Requirements) as a prerequisite check.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Named User](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#named-user) — License Manager section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P06 System Requirements, P50 License Manager, P51 Licensing Options, P04 UI Overview
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact (for activation issues)
Selling Message
0 — Installation guide. No selling message appropriate.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Licensing: Annual, Quarterly, Perpetual, Named User
Languages: English, French, Spanish
Trial: 14 days, full professional features
MINI PRD: P06 — FluidFlow System Requirements & Customer Download Guide
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P3 | Type: Getting Started | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Lists min/recommended specs, Mac compatibility (Boot Camp, VM), customer download steps. ~274 words — very thin. Has broken/garbled link references at the bottom. Missing opening statement, "So What?", glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor
Be the pre-install checklist engineers share with their IT department. Quick scan, clear specs, no ambiguity. Download steps should be foolproof.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement**: "Before installing FluidFlow, confirm your system meets these requirements. Existing customers can download the latest version from the customer portal."
**Clean up broken links**: Lines 476-487 have garbled link text ("fluidflowinfo.com+8knowledge.fluidflowinfo.com+8"). Replace with proper markdown links.
**Expand to 800+ words**: Add detail on what affects performance (model size, number of nodes) and why 2GB RAM is recommended.
**Add Quick Reference table**: Two-column table: Minimum | Recommended for each spec.
**Add "So What?" closing**: "Meeting recommended specs ensures large models calculate without delay."
**Add meta description**.
**Add VM performance note**: When running on a VM, allocate dedicated RAM (not shared) for best performance.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None strongly applicable. This is a system requirements page — glossary links would feel forced. Skip.
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P05 Installation & Activation, P50 License Manager
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
0 — System requirements page. No selling message.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Data & Compatibility: JSON format, FlowDesigner compatibility, PCF enhancements
Languages: English, French, Spanish
MINI PRD: P07 — How to Build a Model in FluidFlow
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P1 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Most complete article in batch 1. 6 steps covering component placement, pipe connection, smart junctions, modification, validation, customization. Includes FAQs. ~1,370 words — already within range. Good detail on auto-junction assignment (2-pipe=bend, 3=tee, 4=cross). Missing opening statement, "So What?", Quick Reference, glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Remain the detailed reference for model building. The content is strong — it mainly needs the PRD-standard wrapper (opening, closing, Quick Reference, glossary links) and a few specificity improvements.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "Building a model in FluidFlow means transforming a physical piping system into a digital network of boundaries, pipes, and equipment. This guide covers component placement, pipe routing, and layout validation — the foundation for every hydraulic calculation."

**Add Quick Reference table**: Key operations table — Place (click palette → click flowsheet), Connect (click source → click destination), Delete (Ctrl+Del), Rotate (Ctrl+R), Resize (Resize tool), Select All (Edit → Select → Select All).
**Add "So What?" closing**: "A well-built model calculates on the first attempt and communicates the design clearly to colleagues — saving hours of debugging and rework."
**Add glossary links** (see below).

**Strengthen specificity**: In Step 2, mention that the Component Palette contains boundaries, pipes, and "14+ valve types, check valves, control valves, relief valves, and general resistance elements" (from spec sheet).
**Add meta description**.
**Minor fix**: Remove "Connector, which supports unlimited pipe connections" — verify this claim against spec sheet. If accurate, keep. If not, tag [VERIFY].
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Flowsheet](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#flowsheet) — Step 1
[Boundaries](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#boundaries) — Step 2
[Component](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#component) — Step 2
[Connectors](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#connectors) — Smart Junction section
[Bend](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#bend) — Smart Junction section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P02 Build Your First Network, P08 How to Enter Input Data, P04 UI Overview, P61-P69 (individual operations)
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Free Training — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
7 — "Short learning curve, deep capability" — fits naturally in the "So What?" closing.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Components: Boundaries (4 types), Pipes (Standard, Rectangular, Annulus, Hose), Fittings (Connectors, Bends, Tees, Cross, Y-junctions)
Valves: 14 types + 5 check valve types
MINI PRD: P08 — How to Enter Input Data
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P2 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Covers boundary conditions, pipe properties, component properties, database expansion (fluids, pumps, control valves, general resistances, solids, insulation). ~1,088 words — within range. Good detail on adding custom data. Missing opening statement, "So What?", Quick Reference, glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Be the definitive data entry reference — the article users return to every time they need to define a new component type. The database expansion section is the real value here and should be emphasized.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "Accurate input data transforms your FluidFlow model from a schematic into a working hydraulic simulation. This guide covers defining boundary conditions, pipe properties, and equipment parameters — and shows you how to extend FluidFlow's built-in database of 1,283 fluids and multi-vendor equipment curves with your own project-specific data."

**Add Quick Reference table**: Input checklist — Boundaries (fluid, pressure/flow, temperature, elevation) → Pipes (material, diameter, length, roughness, elevation) → Equipment (type-specific per component).
**Add "So What?" closing**: "Correct input data means results you can trust for design decisions — and FluidFlow's database means most of that data is already there."
**Add glossary links** (see below).

**Strengthen the database section**: Mention "1,283 fluids" and "5 user-defined fluid types" from spec sheet. Name the 5 types: Simple Newtonian, Pure Newtonian, non-Newtonian Liquid, Gas (No Phase Change), Petroleum Fraction or Crude.
**Add meta description**.
**Add common pitfalls callout**: Most common input errors (wrong units, gauge vs absolute pressure, forgetting elevation).
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Input Editor](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#input-editor) — opening
[Boundaries](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#boundaries) — Step 1
[Pipe](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#pipe) — Step 2
[Absolute Roughness](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#absolute-roughness) — Step 2
[Scaling](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#scaling) — pipe properties
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P07 How to Build a Model, P09 How to Calculate, P17 Creating Fluid Streams, P43 FluidFlow Database
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
5 — "1,283 fluids, 800+ components built in" — naturally fits when discussing the database. One mention, in context.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Databases: 1,283 fluids, 5 user-defined types, 14 pipe materials, multi-vendor equipment curves
Components: all junction types for "component properties" section
Fitting methods: Idelchik, Miller, Crane, SAE (for roughness/resistance context)
MINI PRD: P09 — How to Calculate a Model
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P2 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Covers calculation process, Calculation Options tabs (Current Network, Convergence, Global Settings, Gas, Two-Phase, Slurry, Pulp & Paper). Good specificity — names all 8 two-phase correlations, mentions TAPPI and Moller K. FAQs included. ~890 words — within range. Missing opening statement, "So What?", Quick Reference, glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Be the go-to reference for calculation settings. The correlations section is the strongest part — it should stay prominent. The article should help users understand WHEN to change settings (rarely) and WHAT each setting controls (clearly).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "Clicking Calculate in FluidFlow solves the conservation equations across your entire pipe network — determining flow rates, pressures, temperatures, and velocities at every node. This guide covers the calculation process and the options that control how FluidFlow solves your system."
**Add Quick Reference table**: Calculation Options summary — Tab | What it controls | When to change it | Default recommendation.

**Add "So What?" closing**: "Understanding calculation options means you can trust the default results for standard systems — and know exactly which settings to adjust for specialized applications like high-velocity gas or settling slurry."
**Add glossary links** (see below).

**Verify correlation list completeness**: The two-phase list matches the spec sheet (8 correlations). Confirm Muller-Steinhagen spelling matches spec sheet exactly ("Muller-Steinhagen and Heck" not "Muller-Steinhagen-Heck").
**Add meta description**.

**Strengthen the "when NOT to change settings" message**: The Best Practices already say this but bury it. Make it a prominent callout: "Default settings are validated for typical applications. Change only with specific technical justification."
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Convergence](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#convergence) — Convergence Criteria tab
[Correlation](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#correlation) — Two-Phase section
[Joule-Thomson Effect](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#joule-thomson-effect) — Gas section
[Deposition Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#deposition-velocity) — Slurry section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P08 How to Enter Input Data, P10 How to Analyze Results, P20 Troubleshooting Guide, P55 Results Verification
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
0 — Calculation settings reference. No selling message fits naturally.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Two-Phase correlations: all 8 named
Slurry correlations: settling friction (8), deposition velocity (3 methods)
Pulp & Paper: TAPPI, Moller K
Gas: EOS (3), Joule-Thomson, choked flow detection
Non-Newtonian friction: Darby, Chilton-Stainsby, Converted Power Law
MINI PRD: P10 — How to Analyze Results
Status: Plan
Batch: 1 | Priority: P2 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 800-1500
What This Article Does Now

Covers results viewing, component-specific parameters, visible results customization, flowsheet display, warnings (identification, common types, configuration, engineering judgment), unit customization. Good FAQs. ~1,046 words — within range. Missing opening statement, "So What?", Quick Reference, glossary links. Conclusion section is empty.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Be the article that teaches engineers how to READ FluidFlow output — not just where to find numbers, but what the numbers mean and when to act on warnings. The warnings section is the most valuable part and should be strengthened with the "118 alerts" specificity.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement**: "Running a calculation produces results at every node and pipe in your network — flow rates, pressures, velocities, temperatures, and phase conditions. This guide shows you how to find, interpret, and act on those results, including FluidFlow's 118 built-in design alerts that flag potential issues before they become real-world problems."

**Add Quick Reference table**: Key result parameters with what they mean and when to worry — Velocity (>3 m/s liquid = check erosion), Pressure (below vapor pressure = cavitation risk), NPSH margin (<0.5m = investigate).

**Write the missing Conclusion / "So What?" closing**: "Reading results with engineering judgment — not just accepting green lights — is what separates competent design from box-ticking. FluidFlow's 118 alerts give you a head start, but the final call is yours."
**Add glossary links** (see below).

**Strengthen warnings section**: Mention "118 design alerts, warnings, and hints" from spec sheet. Add example: "A velocity warning at 6.42 m/s in a main header means potential erosion — consider upsizing the pipe or adding a size change."
**Add meta description**.
**Fix the gauge vs absolute pressure callout**: Good content but should be in a prominent callout box, not buried in text. This is a top-3 user mistake.

**Add example interpretation**: Walk through one real result — "Pipe 3 shows 6.42 m/s velocity, which triggers a high-velocity warning. The economic pipe size suggests DN150 vs your current DN100. Here's how to evaluate whether to resize."
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Data Palette](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#data-palette) — Results section
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — understanding results
[Static Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#static-pressure) — gauge vs absolute
[Stagnation Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#stagnation-pressure) — gauge vs absolute
[Cavitation](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#cavitation) — warnings section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P09 How to Calculate, P20 Troubleshooting Guide, P25 How to Export Reports, P55 Results Verification
BLOG: 1 relevant post
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
3 — "Fastest path from problem to answer" — fits naturally when discussing how quickly results appear and how alerts guide the engineer to issues. One mention in context.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Reporting: 118 alerts, configurable velocity/pressure limits, Excel/PDF/BOM
Components: velocity limits for liquid/vapor/two-phase (configurable)
Control valve opening limits (configurable)
MINI PRD: P11 — FluidFlow Designer Handbook
Status: Plan
Batch: 2 | Priority: P11 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2500-3500
What This Article Does Now

Provides a comprehensive introduction to FluidFlow's modeling capabilities with detailed design examples across all five modules (Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase, Slurry, Pulp & Paper). Walks through the user interface, demonstrates boundary conditions and pipe connections, and includes step-by-step liquid modeling exercises with economic velocity sizing. Strong technical foundation with practical examples showing 80% time savings vs Excel.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Transform from tutorial-heavy content into a strategic reference guide that positions FluidFlow as the complete handbook for pipe network design. Retain key examples but add engineering context explaining WHY each methodology matters, WHEN to use each module, and HOW to choose between correlations. Include Quick Reference tables for correlation selection by fluid type and system complexity. Strengthen the "designer's companion" positioning.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that pipe network design requires selecting the right correlation, boundary conditions, and solving methodology — FluidFlow consolidates 40+ years of validated methods into a single designer's handbook.

**Expand module selection guidance** (~400 words): New section explaining which module to use based on fluid properties, expected flow regimes, and system objectives. Include decision tree or selection matrix.
**Add correlation comparison tables**: Quick Reference showing Liquid (Moody, Hazen-Williams, Shell-MIT), Two-Phase (8 correlations with use cases), Slurry (8 methods with particle size ranges).

**Strengthen economic velocity section**: Explain that Generaux equation updates with energy cost data, making FluidFlow's auto-sizing current for 2026 economics (not static formulas from 1980s textbooks).

**Add "Common Pitfalls" section** (~300 words): New users often use Hazen-Williams for non-water fluids, forget heat transfer effects in long gas lines, or skip deposition velocity checks for slurries.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting handbook comprehensiveness to faster project delivery and standards compliance.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Fix module descriptions**: Confirm Scripting Module provides "light" dynamic analysis only — full transient is FlowDesigner (launching 2026).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Economic Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#economic-velocity) — in Economic Velocity sizing section
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in auto-sizing example
[Deposition Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#deposition-velocity) — in Slurry module guidance
[System Curve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#system-curve) — in design methodology section
[NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh) — in pump example validation
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview
SIDEWAYS: P13 Quality Assurance & Standards, P16 Auto-Size Pipes, P07 How to Build a Model, P12 Equipment Sizing
BLOG: Link to "Why Economic Velocity Matters in 2026" (identify at refactor time)
CTA: Free Training — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that complex piping systems often require multiple pumps for reliability, capacity, or head requirements — FluidFlow models parallel pumps (increased flow) and series pumps (increased head) with accurate flow distribution and combined curve generation, ensuring proper pump selection from initial design.

**Add "Parallel vs Series Decision Matrix"** (~350 words): Table showing when to use each configuration. Parallel: High flow demand, redundancy/reliability, duty/standby operation, cooling water systems. Series: High head requirements, multistage booster stations, high-rise buildings, insufficient single-pump head.

**Expand "Parallel Pump Modeling" section** (~500 words): Currently good but add: (1) Flow distribution depends on individual pump curves — unequal curves cause unequal flow split, (2) Each pump sees same head but different flow, (3) NPSH margin must be checked for EACH pump separately, (4) Duty/standby configuration (one runs, one on backup) vs simultaneous operation.

**Expand "Series Pump Modeling" section** (~500 words): Add: (1) Combined head is sum of individual heads at given flow rate, (2) Each pump sees same flow but different suction/discharge pressures, (3) NPSH critical — first pump must not cavitate or second pump sees vapor, (4) Interstage pressure must be reasonable (not excessive).

**Add "Common Mistakes" section** (~400 words): (1) Parallel pumps with mismatched curves (flow imbalance, one pump starves), (2) Series pumps without NPSH check on first pump (cavitation), (3) Duty/standby modeled as simultaneous operation (oversizing), (4) Ignoring check valve resistance in parallel configuration.

**Add "FluidFlow Implementation" subsection** (~300 words): Explain how to build parallel/series models on flowsheet — parallel pumps share common headers, series pumps connected in sequence. FluidFlow automatically combines curves and distributes flow/head.
**Add "Validation Checklist"**: Verify total flow matches sum of parallel pumps, total head matches sum of series pumps, each pump duty point within curve range, NPSH adequate for all pumps.
**Add "Real-World Applications" examples**: Cooling tower systems (parallel pumps for redundancy), high-rise buildings (series booster stations), fire protection (duty/standby jockey pumps).

MINI PRD: P26 — Multi-Component Fluid Mixtures
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P26 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 1800-2500
What This Article Does Now

Guide to modeling multi-component fluid mixtures in FluidFlow, particularly for two-phase systems where multiple pure components (water, hydrocarbons, refrigerants) are mixed. Explains how FluidFlow uses standard mixing rules to calculate mixture physical properties (density, enthalpy, vapor pressure) from component properties. Covers mole fraction vs mass fraction input, flash calculations for phase equilibrium, and how mixture properties affect pressure drop and heat transfer calculations. Includes refrigerant blends and hydrocarbon mixtures.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Strengthen engineering context explaining WHEN mixture modeling is required (refrigerant systems, hydrocarbon processing, chemical plants), HOW FluidFlow handles phase equilibrium (flash calculations at each node), and WHY standard mixing rules are appropriate for most engineering applications. Add validation guidance — when to trust mixing rules vs when rigorous process simulation is needed. Include practical examples: R-410A refrigerant (R-32 + R-125 blend), natural gas mixtures, process condensates. Strengthen connection to real-world two-phase applications.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that industrial piping systems often transport mixtures (refrigerant blends, natural gas, process condensates) where physical properties depend on composition — FluidFlow calculates mixture properties using standard mixing rules and performs flash calculations at each node, ensuring accurate two-phase flow predictions.

**Add "When Mixture Modeling is Required" section** (~300 words): Single-component fluids (pure water, pure propane) use simple property correlations. Multi-component fluids (refrigerant blends, natural gas, hydrocarbon mixtures, chemical process streams) require mixing rules to determine density, enthalpy, vapor pressure, and phase distribution.

**Add "Mixing Rule Background" section** (~350 words): Explain that FluidFlow uses standard mixing rules (Kay's Rule for pseudo-critical properties, mole-weighted mixing for some properties). These are industry-standard methods appropriate for most engineering applications. When rigorous process simulation is needed (highly non-ideal mixtures, chemical reactions), specialized software may be required.

**Expand "Flash Calculations" section** (~400 words): Explain that two-phase systems continuously change composition as light components vaporize and heavy components condense. FluidFlow performs flash calculations (vapor-liquid equilibrium) at each node using selected EOS (Peng-Robinson preferred for hydrocarbons/refrigerants), updating mixture properties as phase fractions change along pipe length.

**Add "Component Input Methods"** (~250 words): Mole fraction (chemists/process engineers typically use this) vs mass fraction (field engineers often prefer this). FluidFlow accepts both, converts internally for calculations.

**Add "Practical Examples" (~400 words): (1) R-410A refrigerant: 50/50 blend of R-32 + R-125, used in HVAC. (2) Natural gas: Methane + ethane + propane + butane + CO₂ + N₂, composition varies by source. (3) Process condensate: Multi-component hydrocarbon liquid from separator, properties change with pressure/temperature.
**Add "EOS Selection for Mixtures"**: Peng-Robinson EOS is generally most accurate for hydrocarbon and refrigerant mixtures. Lee-Kesler for simpler systems. BWRHS for broad property ranges.
**Add "Validation Approach"**: Compare calculated mixture properties (density, vapor pressure) to lab data or literature values at known conditions to validate mixing rule accuracy.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper mixture modeling to accurate two-phase pressure drop and avoiding undersized relief systems.

MINI PRD: P27 — Modeling Positive Displacement Pumps
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P27 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 1500-2000
What This Article Does Now

Guide to modeling positive displacement (PD) pumps in FluidFlow, covering piston, diaphragm, gear, screw, and lobe pump types. Explains how PD pumps deliver nearly constant flow regardless of head (within pressure limits), contrasting with centrifugal pumps where flow varies with system curve. Documents volumetric efficiency modeling, slip losses, and maximum discharge pressure limits. Includes practical examples showing PD pump applications (high-viscosity fluids, precise metering, high-pressure systems).
What It Should Do After Refactor

Strengthen engineering context explaining WHEN PD pumps are preferred over centrifugal (viscous fluids >100 cSt, metering applications, self-priming requirements, high pressure >10 bar), HOW FluidFlow models slip and efficiency losses, and WHY pressure relief protection is mandatory for PD pumps (dead-heading risk). Add decision framework for PD pump type selection. Include validation guidance for volumetric efficiency and maximum pressure rating. Strengthen connection to real-world applications (chemical dosing, hydraulic systems, high-viscosity transfer).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that positive displacement pumps deliver constant flow independent of system resistance (within pressure limits) — FluidFlow models PD pumps with volumetric efficiency corrections and pressure limit enforcement, ensuring accurate performance prediction for metering, high-viscosity, and high-pressure applications.

**Add "PD vs Centrifugal Decision Matrix"** (~400 words): When to use PD pumps — high viscosity (>100 cSt, centrifugal efficiency drops dramatically), precise metering (dosing pumps, chemical injection), self-priming (suction lift, vapor handling), high pressure (>10 bar discharge, centrifugal multi-staging not practical), shear-sensitive fluids (food, polymers). When centrifugal is better — low viscosity, variable flow, lower initial cost.

**Expand "PD Pump Types" section** (~400 words): (1) Reciprocating (piston, plunger, diaphragm): High pressure capability, pulsating flow, lower speed. (2) Rotary (gear, screw, lobe, vane): Smooth flow, moderate pressure, higher speed. Selection based on pressure, viscosity, flow rate, and fluid compatibility.

**Expand "Volumetric Efficiency" section** (~350 words): PD pumps don't deliver theoretical displacement due to slip (internal leakage), compression effects, and fill/discharge valve losses. Efficiency typically 85-98% depending on pump type, speed, viscosity, and pressure rise. FluidFlow models efficiency vs pressure rise for accurate flow prediction.

**Add "Pressure Relief Requirements"** (~300 words): CRITICAL SAFETY POINT — PD pumps can generate unlimited pressure if discharge is blocked (dead-heading). Relief valves or rupture discs MANDATORY on discharge side. FluidFlow warns if calculated discharge pressure exceeds pump maximum rating.

**Add "Slip and Leakage Modeling"**: Internal slip increases with pressure differential and decreases with viscosity (thick fluids seal clearances better). FluidFlow accounts for slip-based efficiency reduction.

**Add "Applications Examples"** (~300 words): Chemical dosing (diaphragm pumps, precise flow), hydraulic systems (piston pumps, high pressure), food processing (lobe pumps, sanitary design, shear-sensitive), crude oil transfer (screw pumps, high viscosity).

**Add "Validation Checklist"**: Verify discharge pressure within pump rating, efficiency appropriate for viscosity and pressure, relief valve sized adequately, NPSH adequate (PD pumps still require NPSHa > NPSHr).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper PD pump modeling to avoiding safety hazards and accurate metering performance.
**Add glossary links and internal linking** (see below).

MINI PRD: P29 — Viscosity Correction for Centrifugal Pumps
Status: Plan
Batch: 3 | Priority: P29 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 1800-2500
What This Article Does Now

Technical guide on how viscosity affects centrifugal pump performance and how FluidFlow applies correction factors. Explains that centrifugal pumps are typically rated on water (1 cP viscosity) but performance degrades with higher-viscosity fluids due to increased internal friction and reduced turbulence. Documents viscosity correction methods available in FluidFlow for adjusting head, flow, and efficiency. Includes guidance on when corrections are necessary (typically above 20 cSt viscosity).
What It Should Do After Refactor

Strengthen engineering context explaining WHY viscosity corrections matter (ignoring corrections can result in 30-50% flow reduction for oils, syrups, polymers), WHEN corrections are required (typically >20 cSt, mandatory >100 cSt), and HOW FluidFlow implements industry-standard correction methods. Add viscosity threshold guidance and practical examples showing performance degradation. Include validation approach (compare corrected curves to manufacturer viscous-fluid test data). Strengthen connection to avoiding pump under-performance failures.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State that centrifugal pump curves are published for water (1 cP viscosity) but performance degrades significantly for viscous fluids (oils, syrups, polymers) — FluidFlow applies industry-standard viscosity corrections ensuring accurate duty point prediction and avoiding undersized pump selections.

**Add "Viscosity Effects on Pump Performance" section** (~400 words): Explain physical mechanisms — viscous fluids increase internal recirculation losses, reduce turbulence (lower efficiency), increase friction in impeller passages (lower head). Performance degradation is negligible below 20 cSt, moderate 20-100 cSt (5-15% head reduction), severe above 100 cSt (can exceed 50% flow reduction). FluidFlow warns when viscosity corrections should be applied.

**Add "When Corrections Are Required" table**: <20 cSt (water-like, corrections optional), 20-100 cSt (moderate viscosity, corrections recommended), >100 cSt (high viscosity, corrections mandatory), >1000 cSt (very high, centrifugal pumps may not be suitable, consider PD pumps per P27).

**Expand "Correction Methods" section** (~400 words): FluidFlow implements standard viscosity correction procedures (likely Hydraulic Institute standards or manufacturer methods — verify against spec sheet). Corrections adjust head-flow curve, efficiency curve, and power curve. Corrected curves show reduced flow at same head, lower efficiency at all points, shifted BEP location.

**Add "Practical Examples" (~400 words): (1) Lube oil transfer (SAE 30 oil, ~100 cSt at 40°C): Water curve shows 100 m³/h at 30m head, viscosity correction reduces to ~85 m³/h at 30m head, efficiency drops from 75% to 65%. (2) Hydraulic fluid (ISO VG 68, ~68 cSt at 40°C): Moderate corrections apply. (3) Heavy syrup (500 cSt): Severe corrections, centrifugal pump may not be viable.

**Add "Temperature Dependence"**: Viscosity strongly temperature-dependent (oil viscosity can vary 10× between 20°C and 80°C). FluidFlow accounts for temperature effects on viscosity and recalculates corrections at actual operating temperature.

**Add "Validation Approach"** (~250 words): If manufacturer provides viscous-fluid test curves, compare FluidFlow corrected curve to manufacturer data. Typical agreement within ±5% for validated correction methods.
**Add "When to Consider PD Pumps"**: Above ~1000 cSt, centrifugal efficiency becomes very poor. Positive displacement pumps (P27) may be more suitable for very high viscosity applications.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting proper viscosity correction to avoiding pump under-performance and system flow shortfalls.

Serve as the authoritative guide for designing FluidFlow-compatible systems — the article experienced users bookmark. Open with the purpose (avoiding common modeling pitfalls, designing for accurate results), organize practices by category (network topology, boundary conditions, data quality, convergence, validation), and explain WHY each practice matters.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (best practices for accurate, stable FluidFlow models), what the reader will learn, and name FluidFlow directly.
**Expand to deep-dive depth** (~2,000 words): Sections on network topology, boundary conditions, data quality, convergence strategies, validation methods, performance optimization, common mistakes.

**Add "Network Topology Best Practices" section**: Simplify complex networks (break into zones), avoid redundant components, use connectors wisely (zero resistance), model only what matters (omit negligible resistances), use appropriate detail level (factor method vs. isometric).

**Add "Boundary Condition Best Practices" section**: One boundary per independent path, avoid over-constraining (too many fixed pressures/flows), use realistic values (field measurements > assumptions), consider boundary location (far enough from area of interest).

**Add "Data Quality Best Practices" section**: Use vendor data when available, verify fluid properties (temperature, pressure, composition), check pipe roughness (new vs. aged), validate resistance coefficients (K-factors, Cv values), measure when possible (flow meters, pressure gauges).

**Add "Convergence Strategies" section**: Start simple (add complexity incrementally), check for closed loops (need flow boundary), verify boundary conditions (no conflicts), adjust relaxation factors (if available), break into sub-problems (isolate non-converging zones) — link to P20 Troubleshooting Guide.

**Add "Validation Methods" section**: Cross-check with hand calcs (simple sections), compare to field data (if available), use QA test models (verify methodology), check mass/energy balance (should close), review alerts/warnings (118 built-in checks) — link to P55 Results Verification.

**Add "Performance Optimization" section**: Simplify network topology (reduce node count), use appropriate solver settings, leverage multi-calc for scenarios (vs. separate files) — link to P22 Multi-Calc.

**Add "Common Mistakes to Avoid" section**: Over-constraining boundary conditions, ignoring warnings, using default values without verification, modeling excessive detail, not validating results, forgetting heat effects (when significant).

**Add "Design Phase vs. Analysis Phase" section**: Design phase (iterate quickly, use auto-sizing, explore alternatives), Analysis phase (validate with field data, refine details, document assumptions).

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Filter presses start each cycle with low resistance (clean filter cloths), then build pressure as cake accumulates. Pumps sized for mid-cycle conditions either stall at end-of-cycle or waste energy at start. FluidFlow's pump curve analysis models this resistance change, showing duty point drift across the cycle.
**Pain point mapping**: Fixed pump sizing → duty point drift as filter resistance increases → stall (flow drop) or energy waste → inefficient cycle times → higher operating cost.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid or Slurry Module pump curve modeling, system curve overlay, duty point tracking across resistance range. Show how changing filter K-factor (0.5 to 5.0 as cake builds) shifts duty point along pump curve.

**Proof element**: Model filter-press feed pump at three cycle stages: clean (K=0.5), mid-cycle (K=2.0), end-cycle (K=5.0). Show duty point shift from 120 m³/h @ 8 bar to 80 m³/h @ 12 bar. Demonstrate pump selection that stays within curve limits across full range.

When basin level drops during high evaporation, NPSHa falls below NPSHr, causing cavitation and emergency trips. FluidFlow's NPSH analysis models worst-case low level, ensuring pumps remain cavitation-free.
**Pain point mapping**: Low basin level → insufficient NPSHa → cavitation → pump trip → cooling water shortage → equipment overheating → unplanned shutdown.

**FluidFlow capability**: Liquid Module NPSH calculation (NPSHa vs NPSHr), pump database with NPSHr curves, automatic cavitation alert (one of 118 design alerts), scenario analysis for low-level conditions.

**Proof element**: Model cooling tower make-up pump at two basin levels: normal (3.5m static head, NPSHa = 3.2m, NPSHr = 2.8m, margin = 0.4m) vs low (2.0m static head, NPSHa = 1.5m, NPSHr = 2.8m, CAVITATION). Demonstrate correction: select low-NPSHr pump (NPSHr = 1.2m) or increase basin depth to 3.0m minimum.

**Glossary links** (first use): [NPSH](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#npsh), [Cavitation](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#cavitation), [Static Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#static-head), [Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point).

**Opening statement** (2-3 sentences): Chemical plants run pumps in parallel, staging them (1-pump for small batches, 2-pump for normal, 3-pump for peak) to match demand. FluidFlow's parallel pump analysis models each staging scenario, ensuring stable operation across all combinations.

Demonstrate how to select pumps that stay within efficiency/velocity limits across all staging scenarios.
